narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical student's 3.21. However, the admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted an 84.9% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, leading to the tough decision to reject your application right now. This prediction hinges heavily on your early academic performance in school—specifically, your placement in the lowest decile for grades in both Year 1 and Year 3—which carried the strongest weight in pushing the model toward forecasting failure, well below what most admitted students achieve. Your LSAT score of 27 also played a notable role in that direction, falling short of the average around 37 that the model associates with bar success. On a brighter note, your above-average undergrad GPA worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing, while factors like your middle-family income bracket and full-time student status had smaller influences that didn't shift the overall outcome much. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early decile rankings are a standout concern, as they're much lower than the mid-range averages of about 5.5 to 5.7, suggesting the model sees them as key hurdles to long-term success. Your full-time status aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%, so it didn't help or hurt significantly. We know this rejection stings, especially given the barriers many Black women like you face in higher education, but it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting those early-grade deciles through targeted academic support or retakes if possible, aim to retake the LSAT for a higher score, and highlight any additional achievements that demonstrate growth. Consider community programs or prep courses tailored for underrepresented students to build your bar exam readiness. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing, and reapply when you've addressed these core areas. We're rooting for you.
